topic.what: Characterisation of bile reflux as a cause of distinct chemical (reactive) gastropathy, separate from H. pylori-associated gastritis, with potential contribution to Barrett's oesophagus development
topic.how: Cohort study comparing histological and clinical features of patients with documented duodenogastric reflux against H. pylori-positive and normal controls, grading foveolar hyperplasia, oedema, smooth-muscle proliferation, and inflammatory infiltrate
topic.why_matters: Establishes bile reflux gastropathy as a distinct clinicopathological entity with its own histological signature, informing differential diagnosis and highlighting a non-acid pathway to Barrett's metaplasia

characteristics.what_it_measures: Ability to distinguish chemical gastropathy from H. pylori gastritis by histological features
characteristics.value: distinct pattern of foveolar hyperplasia, oedema, smooth-muscle proliferation with minimal inflammation
characteristics.vs_baseline: clearly distinguishable from H. pylori-associated chronic active gastritis
characteristics.context: Surgical and endoscopic cohort with confirmed duodenogastric reflux

good_for: differential diagnosis of non-H. pylori gastropathy

novelty: Systematic delineation of bile reflux gastropathy as a histologically distinct entity from H. pylori gastritis
Proposed mechanistic link between chronic bile reflux and Barrett's oesophagus via chemical mucosal injury
open_problems: Quantitative bile acid thresholds for gastropathy induction remain undefined
Prospective studies needed to confirm bile reflux as independent Barrett's risk factor
Optimal management strategies for bile reflux gastropathy are unresolved
